content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 2, 2016

Julie Chapman
Legal Services Supervisor
Mendocino County Counsel Office
501 Low Gap Road, Rm 1030
Ukiah, CA 95482

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-16-132

Dear Ms. Chapman:

This letter responds to your request for assistance on behalf of Mendocino County regarding your required AB1234 training which includes provisions of Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note that our assistance is limited to the provisions of the Act and Section 1090,² we do not review portions of the training that pertain to laws outside of our jurisdiction (See Regulation 18371), and we do not have any enforcement authority with respect to Section 53235 or other provisions of AB 1234. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.³

With respect to the other laws covered by the training (slides 1-7, 9-13, 16-17, 22, 24-33, 35-43, and 46-50), you may want to contact the Attorney General's Office for their feedback. Please note that the Attorney General's Office has its own requirements for AB1234 training.⁴

Discussion

Section 53235(a), enacted by Assembly Bill No. 1234 (2005-2006 Reg. Sess.), requires certain local agency officials to receive training in ethics. Regulation 18371 provides that "the topics enumerated below . . . are considered to be the core content of the 'ethics laws' component that pertain to the Political Reform Act, and regarding which, the Commission will provide consultation. The course should include instruction on each of the following topics if relevant to the official's public position:"

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 and 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Section 1090 is not in the Act. However, in 2013, Assembly Bill 1090 granted the Commission the authority to provide written advice on Section 1090.

³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

⁴ http://oag.ca.gov/sites/all/files/agweb/pdfs/ethics/eth_loc_guide_final.pdf?


File No. 1-16-132
Page No. 2

"(1) Conflicts of Interest under the Political Reform Act (Gov. Code §§
87100, 87103).

"(2) Conflicts of Interest and Campaign Contributions (Gov. Code §
84308).

"(3) Limitations on the Receipt of Gifts (Gov. Code §§ 86203, 89503,
89506).

"(4) Honoraria Ban (Gov. Code § 89502).

"(5) Conflicts of Interest When Leaving Office (Gov. Code §§ 87406.1,
87406.3, 87407).

"(6) Mass Mailing Restrictions (Gov. Code § 89001).

"(7) Economic Interest Disclosure Under the Political Reform Act (Gov.
Code § 87200 et seq.)."

The standard we apply reviewing the training is twofold: (1) has the training covered the
required topics, and (2) has the training sufficiently and effectively put your officials on notice of
their responsibilities under the covered laws. While you have provided power-point slides that
touch on each of the listed topics, we do not believe that the discussions are sufficient detailed to
inform officials of their responsibilities under the law.⁵ Therefore, we have attempted to add
information to your slides that will provide a more substantive summary of the specific laws and we
have suggested handouts.

General Considerations

1. From a legal perspective, many (if not all) of the specific terms in the statutes are
significant. We actually further define many of the terms in our regulations and establish tests
regarding them. Consequently, we would caution omitting terms that are important parts of the
analysis. Where appropriate we have readded terms that we think are important to the analysis.

2. Some of the discussion in the slides do not fully cover the topics. You should supplement
where you can with handouts, many of which are available on our website. We have also added
some discussion which you may find useful. In addition, we host an AB 1234 training on our
website.⁶ You may want to use the content of some of those slides to supplement the information
you are providing to your officials.

___________________________

⁵ Of course, we do not know if you will be adding greater detail orally during the training.

⁶ http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules- ethics-training.html


File No. 1-16-132
Page No. 3

Specific Suggestions

We suggest the following changes which are delineated in underscore and strikeout font. Explanation will generally be in text or in a footnote.

Slide 8, Bullet 2

Rule: As a public official, you may not make, participate in making, or use your official position to influence a decision if financial interests are affected by a decision the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on you or your financial interests. 7

Slide 14

B. Conflicts of Interest Pursuant to the PRA
Political Reform Act (PRA): Initiative passed by the voters in 1974; administered by the FPPC. (Gov't Code §§ 87100 – 87103)

■ "Strong public policy: Insure that public officials "perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial gain…"

■ "General rule: You are not to participate in, or use your official position to influence, a governmental decision in which you or a family member have a financial interest unless (1) your participation is legally required, or, (2) the effect of the decision is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

– "Has to be a financial interest or a conflict arising from particular kinds of economic interests.

■ "Restatement of the general rule: You have a conflict of interest in a given decision if it is likely that the outcome of the decision will have a significant impact on your personal economic interests that is greater than the impact on the public in general." 8

Slide 15

The COI rules of the PRA only apply to conflicts that arise from five specific eeonomic financial 9 interests: business entities; real property, sources of income to the public official; sources of gifts to the public official; and the "personal financial effects" rule. 10

7 See General Consideration #1 above.

8 This bullet attempts to reword the Act's general conflict-of-interest prohibition using different terms than those used in the Act, potentially leading to confusion (see General Consideration 1 above). Since you state the rule in the second bullet, we recommend that you delete this restatement.

9 The recent version of the regulations redefines the interests in Section 87103 as "financial interests." (Regulation 18700(c)(6).) "Economic interest" should be changed to "financial interest" wherever it appears in the slides.

10 [illegible]


File No. 1-16-132
Page No. 4

Examples:
- You, or an immediate family member, or a business in which you are a 10% or more owner, has a financial or economic interest in the decision. (Gov't Code § 87102)¹¹
- Direct or indirect investment or more than of $2,000 or more in a business, or employment by the business.¹²
- Direct or indirect interest in real property worth more than $2,000 or more (fair market value).
- Income source of greater than $500 or more within 12 months prior to the relevant governmental decision.
- Sources of gifts of greater than $460 or more (the Commission increases the threshold biennially increases with for inflation based on the consumer price index) from a donor within 12 months prior to the relevant governmental decision.¹³
- Financial effect on your finances or those of your family.
- For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

Slide 18

This slide discusses aspects of Section 1090 without stating the prohibition itself. We would suggest adding a slide right before this one that includes the statutory language.

"Members of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members. Nor shall state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees be purchasers at any sale or vendors at any purchase made by them in their official capacity."

We would then suggest modifying slide 18 (or 18a) to add a bit more detail as follows:

C. Warning!
Special Rules for Contracts
(Gov't Code § 1090 et seq.)

¹⁰ We also provide a conflict of interest law fact sheet on our website. http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules- conflict-of-Interest.html

¹¹ This appears to be from the "indirect interest" definition in 87103. We would add the term "indirect interest." The placement at the end of the list is the more standard placement.

¹² The thresholds in Section 87103 are generally "[§ threshold] or more." So, for example, a gift of $460 is potentially disqualifying

¹³ Clarifying that the "source" of the gift is the interests, similar to the income source.


File No. 1-16-132
Page No. 5

■ Disqualification may not be enough
■ Section 1090 applies to direct or indirect interest interests in a contract
■ There are only limited exceptions. Exceptions to Section 1090's general
prohibition for "remote interests" are set forth in Section 1091, and exceptions
for "noninterests" are set forth in Section 1091.5.
■ In some cases, Section 1090 not only prohibits the financially interested officer
from making or participating in the making of the public contract, it also
prohibits the governing body of the public entity in which the officer is a
member from entering into the contract. (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 50 (2006).)
■ Potential consequences of a violation of Section 1090:
___May have to refund money paid
___Felony: $1,000 fine, imprisonment, and loss of office
– A contract made in violation of Section 1090 is void and unenforceable.
(See Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633.)
– A contract in violation of Section 1090 will result in disgorgement of the
contract benefits. (See County of San Bernardino v. Walsh (2007) 158
Cal.App.4th 533.)
– A willful violation of Section 1090 is punishable by a fine of up to $1,000
or imprisonment in the state prison. (Section 1097.)

Slide 19

D. COI & Campaign
Contributions

No officer…shall accept, solicit, or direct a contribution of more than two
hundred fifty dollars ($250)…while a proceeding involving a license, permit, or
other entitlement for use is pending before the agency…

(Gov. Code § 84308)

We would suggest instead the following which is taken from our fact-sheet available on
our website:14

Section 84308 contains three restrictions:

A party, or his or her agent, seeking a license, permit, or other entitlement
for use may not contribute more than $250 to an officer of the agency while the
proceeding is pending and for three months after.

An officer of an agency may not solicit a contribution of more than $250
from a party or participant to a proceeding, or his or her agent, while the
proceeding is pending and for three months after.

14 http: www.fppc.ca.gov learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules- conflict-of-interest/pay-to-play-
contribution-restrictions.html


File No. I-16-132
Page No. 6

An officer of an agency may not participate in a decision on a license, permit, or other entitlement for use if the officer has received a contribution of more than $250 from a party, participant or his or her agent.

Slide 20

Local officials are subject to a different form of post-governmental employment restriction. This language is from our factsheet (Leaving Local Government Employment) and is availabe on our website.15 Again, using the fact-sheet as a handout is suggested.

■ The Political Reform Act places two restrictions on the post-governmental activity of officials who leave local governmental service. These restrictions are:
– A one-year ban applicable to high-level local officials (Section 87406.3) and
– A ban on influencing prospective employment, prohibits current local officials from taking part in decisions that directly relate to a prospective employer. (Section 87407.)

Slide 21

Best Practices

■ Avoid temptation to look at public service as an opportunity for financial gain

■ Look at every decision and ask yourself whether it involves some kind of financial interest for you

■ Seek advice from the Mendocino County Counsel or the Commission if you suspect you have a conflict of interest with respect to a decision.16

Slide 23

This slide does not provide sufficient information to assist your officials in avoiding gift limit violations or avoid prohibited honoraria. We would suggest you use the gift fact sheet, available on our website.17 With respect to the slides, we suggest (1) using the following language from the factsheet to create a series of slides, or (2) you can shorten the discussion and use the factsheet as a handout. We have included additional text for you to use under option 2.

A. __ Limitations on receipt of gifts

15 http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules- rules-on-leaving-government-service.html

16 Because of the complexity of the laws, we always suggest they get legal assistance when questions arise.

17 http://www.fppc.ca.gov/content/dam/fppc/NS-Documents/TAD/Public%20Officials%20and%20Employees LocalGiftFactSheet.pdf


File No. 1-16-132
Page No. 7

(Gov't Code §§ 86203, 89503, 89506)
-Meals, food and drink (including receptions)
-Entertainment (concerts & sporting events)
-Certain kinds of travel and lodging
-Acceptance of free or discounted transportation
(Cal. Constit., art XII, § 7)

B. Honoraria Ban (Gov't Code — Section 89502)
A payment for a service (as making a speech) on which custom or propriety forbids a price to be set

Option 1

$460 Gift Limit (Effective January 1, 2015 - December 31, 2016): State and local officials
and employees are prohibited from receiving a gift or gifts totaling more than $460 in a
calendar year from certain sources. For elected state officials and many others, the
prohibition is applicable to gifts from any source, although there are exceptions (for
example, gifts from family members). For state and local officials and employees who file
Statements of Economic Interests (Form 700s) under an agency's conflict of interest code,
the gift limit is applicable only to individuals and entities that would have to be disclosed on
the Form 700. This gift limit is adjusted for inflation every odd-numbered year. (Note:
Judges are not subject to the Act's gift prohibitions, but are covered by the Code of Civil
Procedure.)

Honoraria: An honorarium is a payment received for making a speech, publishing an article
or attending any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal or
similar gathering. State and local elected officers and candidates for those offices and all
officials holding positions listed in Government Code Section 87200 are prohibited from
receiving honoraria payments. Likewise, an employee designated under a state or local
government agency's conflict of interest code is prohibited from receiving honoraria
payments from any source of gifts or income the employee is required to report on his or her
Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700). Some limited exceptions apply, such as
income earned from a bona fide business or profession.

Exceptions for Travel: Certain payments for travel are excluded from the gift limits and
honoraria prohibition. Refer to the appropriate gift fact sheet to determine if your travel
payment is subject to the gift limit.

Option 2. Abbreviated discussion to be used in conjunction with handing out the fact sheet.

Limitations on receipt of gifts and honoraria
(Gov't Code §89503 and §89506)

■ Gifts: A gift is a payment made by any person of any thing of value that
provides a personal benefit to an official when the official does not provide full
consideration for the value of the benefit received. (Regulation 18940(a).) Local
elected officers, candidates for local elective office, specified local officials, and
designated employees of a local government agency may not accept a gift or gifts


File No. l-16-132
Page No. 8

from any single source totaling more than $460 in a calendar year. (Section
895(B.)
■ Honoraria: An honorarium is any payment made in consideration for a speech
given, article published, or attendance at any public or private event, meal, or
similar gathering. (Section 89501.) Specified local officials and designated
employees of local agencies may not receive honoraria payments. (Section
89502.)

Slide 27, Bullet 2

It would b e useful to show the definition of mass mailing so that your officials can be alert to avoid
violating the law.

■ Mass mailing restrictions (GC § 89001) Goal: restrict incumbents' advantages.

■ A mailing is prohibited by Section 89001 if all of the following criteria are met:

■ The item is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business, or post office box. The item delivered to the recipient
must be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written
document.

■ The item sent either: (A) features an elected officer affiliated with the agency, or (B)
includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or
sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer.

■ (A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or (B) Costs of
design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and
the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other
than as permitted by this regulation.

■ More than two hundred substantially y similar items are sent, in a single calendar
month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request or other
exception.

Slides 44-45

These slides set forth the eight-step analysis formerly applied to determine whether a public official
is disqualified from taking part in a decision under Section 87100. In 2015, the Commission
overhauled its conflict-of-interest regulations, abandoned this eight-step analysis, and replaced it
with a four-step analysis set forth in Regulation 18700(d). Therefore, we recommend deleting these
slides.

To the extent that you do want to discuss the 4-step process, we would advise it be addressed
near adjacent to Slide 14. Here's some suggested language on the four-step process.


File No. 1-16-132
Page No. 9

Conflict-of-Interest Analysis Under the Political Reform Act
(Regulation 18700(d))

■ Step One: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a
financial effect on the public official's financial interests? (Apply Regulation
18701.)

■ Step Two: Will the reasonably foreseeable financial effect be material? (Apply
Regulation 18702.)

■ Step Three: Can the public official demonstrate that the material financial effect
on the official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally? (Apply Regulation 18703.)

■ Step Four: Is there an applicable exception? If not the official is prohibited from
making, participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official position
to influence the decision. If disqualified, an official may be required to comply
with the Act's requirements for disqualification set forth in Regulation 18707.

We hope this information is of assistance. If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

Section 53235(a), enacted by Assembly Bill No. 1234 (2005-2006 Reg. Sess.), requires certain local agency officials to receive training in ethics. Regulation 18371 provides that “the topics enumerated below . . . are considered to be the core content of the ‘ethics laws’ component that pertain to² Section 1090 is not in the Act. However, in 2013, Assembly Bill 1090 granted the Commission the authority to provide written advice on Section 1090.

³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

⁴ http://oag.ca.gov/sites/all/files/agweb/pdfs/ethics/eth_loc_guide_final.pdf?

"(1) Conflicts of Interest under the Political Reform Act (Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103).

"(2) Conflicts of Interest and Campaign Contributions (Gov. Code § 84308).

"(3) Limitations on the Receipt of Gifts (Gov. Code §§ 86203, 89503, 89506).

"(4) Honoraria Ban (Gov. Code § 89502).

"(5) Conflicts of Interest When Leaving Office (Gov. Code §§ 87406.1, 87406.3, 87407).

"(6) Mass Mailing Restrictions (Gov. Code § 89001).

"(7) Economic Interest Disclosure Under the Political Reform Act (Gov. Code § 87200 et seq.)."

The standard we apply reviewing the training is twofold: (1) has the training covered the required topics, and (2) has the training sufficiently and effectively put your officials on notice of their responsibilities under the covered laws. While you have provided power-point slides that touch on each of the listed topics, we do not believe that the discussions are sufficient detailed to inform officials of their responsibilities under the law.5 Therefore, we have attempted to add information to your slides that will provide a more substantive summary of the specific laws and we have suggested handouts.

General Considerations

1. From a legal perspective, many (if not all) of the specific terms in the statutes are significant. We actually further define many of the terms in our regulations and establish tests regarding them. Consequently, we would caution omitting terms that are important parts of the analysis. Where appropriate we have readded terms that we think are important to the analysis.

2. Some of the discussion in the slides do not fully cover the topics. You should supplement where you can with handouts, many of which are available on our website. We have also added some discussion which you may find useful. In addition, we host an AB 1234 training on our website.6 You may want to use the content of some of those slides to supplement the information you are providing to your officials.

5 Of course, we do not know if you will be adding greater detail orally during the training.
6 http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules-ethics-training.html

Specific Suggestions

We suggest the following changes which are delineated in underscore and strikeout font. Explanation will generally be in text or in a footnote.

Slide 8, Bullet 2

Rule: As a public official, you may not make, participate in making, or use your official position to influence a decision if financial interests are affected by a decision—the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on you or your financial interests.7

Slide 14

B. Conflicts of Interest Pursuant to the PRA
Political Reform Act (PRA): Initiative passed by the voters in 1974; administered by the FPPC. (Gov’t Code §§ 87100 – 87103)

■ “Strong public policy: Insure that public officials “perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial gain…””

■ “General rule: You are not to participate in, or use your official position to influence, a governmental decision in which you or a family member have a financial interest unless (1) your participation is legally required, or, (2) the effect of the decision is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
    – “Has to be a financial interest or a conflict arising from particular kinds of economic interests.

■ “Restatement of the general rule: You have a conflict of interest in a given decision if it is likely that the outcome of the decision will have a significant impact on your personal economic interests that is greater than the impact on the public in general.”8

Slide 15

The COI rules of the PRA only apply to conflicts that arise from five specific economic financial9 interests: business entities; real property, sources of income to the public official; sources of gifts to the public official; and the “personal financial effects” rule.10

7 See General Consideration #1 above.

8 This bullet attempts to reword the Act’s general conflict-of-interest prohibition using different terms than those used in the Act, potentially leading to confusion (see General Consideration 1 above). Since you state the rule in the second bullet, we recommend that you delete this restatement.

9 The recent version of the regulations redefines the interests in Section 87103 as “financial interests.” (Regulation 18700(c)(6).) “Economic interest” should be changed to “financial interest” wherever it appears in the slides.

Examples:
- You, or an immediate family member, or a business in which you are a 10% or more owner, has a financial or economic interest in the decision. (Gov’t Code § 87103)11
- Direct or indirect investment of more than $2,000 or more in a business, or employment by the business.12
- Direct or indirect interest in real property worth more than $2,000 or more (fair market value).
- Income source of greater than $500 or more within 12 months prior to the relevant governmental decision.
- Sources of gifts of greater than $460 or more (the Commission increases the threshold biennially increases with for inflation based on the consumer price index) from a donor within 12 months prior to the relevant governmental decision.13
- Financial effect on your finances or those of your family.
- For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official’s agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

Slide 18

This slide discusses aspects of Section 1090 without stating the prohibition itself. We would suggest adding a slide right before this one that includes the statutory language.

“Members of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members. Nor shall state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees be purchasers at any sale or vendors at any purchase made by them in their official capacity.”

We would then suggest modifying slide 18 (or 18a) to add a bit more detail as follows:

C. Warning!
Special Rules for Contracts
(Gov’t Code § 1090 et seq.)

11 We also provide a conflict of interest law fact sheet on our website. http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules-conflict-of-interest.html

12 This appears to be from the “indirect interest” definition in 87103. We would add the term “indirect interest.” The placement at the end of the list is the more standard placement.

■ Disqualification may not be enough
■ Section 1090 applies to direct or indirect interest interests in a contract
■ There are only limited exceptions. Exceptions to Section 1090’s general prohibition for “remote interests” are set forth in Section 1091, and exceptions for “noninterests” are set forth in Section 1091.5.
■ In some cases, Section 1090 not only prohibits the financially interested officer from making or participating in the making of the public contract, it also prohibits the governing body of the public entity in which the officer is a member from entering into the contract. (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 50 (2006).)
■ Potential consequences of a violation of Section 1090:
   — May have to refund money paid
   — Felony: $1,000 fine, imprisonment, and loss of office
   — A contract made in violation of Section 1090 is void and unenforceable. (See Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633.)
   — A contract in violation of Section 1090 will result in disgorgement of the contract benefits. (See County of San Bernardino v. Walsh (2007) 158 Cal.App.4th 533.)
   — A willful violation of Section 1090 is punishable by a fine of up to $1,000 or imprisonment in the state prison. (Section 1097.)

Slide 19

D. COI & Campaign Contributions

No officer...shall accept, solicit, or direct a contribution of more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250)...while a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is pending before the agency...

(Gov. Code § 84308)

We would suggest instead the following which is taken from our fact-sheet available on our website:14

Section 84308 contains three restrictions:

A party, or his or her agent, seeking a license, permit, or other entitlement for use may not contribute more than $250 to an officer of the agency while the proceeding is pending and for three months after.

An officer of an agency may not solicit a contribution of more than $250 from a party or participant to a proceeding, or his or her agent, while the proceeding is pending and for three months after.

14 http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules-conflict-of-interest/pay-to-play-contribution-restrictions.html

An officer of an agency may not participate in a decision on a license, permit, or other entitlement for use if the officer has received a contribution of more than $250 from a party, participant or his or her agent.

Slide 20

Local officials are subject to a different form of post-governmental employment restriction. This language is from our factsheet (Leaving Local Government Employment) and is available on our website.15 Again, using the fact-sheet as a handout is suggested.

■ The Political Reform Act places two restrictions on the post-governmental activity of officials who leave local governmental service. These restrictions are:
   – A one-year ban applicable to high-level local officials (Section 87406.3) and
   – A ban on influencing prospective employment, prohibits current local officials from taking part in decisions that directly relate to a prospective employer. (Section 87407.)

Slide 21

Best Practices

■ Avoid temptation to look at public service as an opportunity for financial gain

■ Look at every decision and ask yourself whether it involves some kind of financial interest for you

■ Seek advice from the Mendocino County Counsel or the Commission if you suspect you have a conflict of interest with respect to a decision.16

This slide does not provide sufficient information to assist your officials in avoiding gift limit violations or avoid prohibited honoraria. We would suggest you use the gift fact sheet, available on our website.17 With respect to the slides, we suggest (1) using the following language from the factsheet to create a series of slides, or (2) you can shorten the discussion and use the factsheet as a handout. We have included additional text for you to use under option 2.

A. Limitations on receipt of gifts

15 http://www.fppc.ca.gov/learn/public-officials-and-employees-rules/rules-on-leaving-government-service.html

16 Because of the complexity of the laws, we always suggest they get legal assistance when questions arise.

17 http://www.fppc.ca.gov/content/dam/fppc/NS-Documents/TAD_Public%20Officials%20and%20Employees/LocalGiftFactSheet.pdf

(Gov’t Code §§ 86203, 89503, 89506)
– Meals, food and drink (including receptions)
– Entertainment (concerts & sporting events)
– Certain kinds of travel and lodging
– Acceptance of free or discounted transportation
(Cal. Const., art XII, § 7)

B.—Honoraria Ban (Gov’t Code—Section 89502)
A payment for a service (as making a speech) on which custom or propriety forbids a price to be set

Option 1

$460 Gift Limit (Effective January 1, 2015 - December 31, 2016): State and local officials and employees are prohibited from receiving a gift or gifts totaling more than $460 in a calendar year from certain sources. For elected state officials and many others, the prohibition is applicable to gifts from any source, although there are exceptions (for example, gifts from family members). For state and local officials and employees who file Statements of Economic Interests (Form 700s) under an agency’s conflict of interest code, the gift limit is applicable only to individuals and entities that would have to be disclosed on the Form 700. This gift limit is adjusted for inflation every odd-numbered year. (Note: Judges are not subject to the Act’s gift prohibitions, but are covered by the Code of Civil Procedure.)

Honoraria: An honorarium is a payment received for making a speech, publishing an article or attending any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal or similar gathering. State and local elected officers and candidates for those offices and all officials holding positions listed in Government Code Section 87200 are prohibited from receiving honoraria payments. Likewise, an employee designated under a state or local government agency’s conflict of interest code is prohibited from receiving honoraria payments from any source of gifts or income the employee is required to report on his or her Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700). Some limited exceptions apply, such as income earned from a bona fide business or profession.

Exceptions for Travel: Certain payments for travel are excluded from the gift limits and honoraria prohibition. Refer to the appropriate gift fact sheet to determine if your travel payment is subject to the gift limit.

Option 2. Abbreviated discussion to be used in conjunction with handing out the fact sheet.

Limitations on receipt of gifts and honoraria
(Gov’t Code §§89503 and §89506)

■ Gifts: A gift is a payment made by any person of any thing of value that provides a personal benefit to an official when the official does not provide full consideration for the value of the benefit received. (Regulation 18940(a).) Local elected officers, candidates for local elective office, specified local officials, and designated employees of a local government agency may not accept a gift or gifts

The image is completely white, with no visible content or details to analyze or describe.

from any single source totaling more than $460 in a calendar year. (Section 89503.)

■ Honoraria: An honorarium is any payment made in consideration for a speech given, article published, or attendance at any public or private event, meal, or similar gathering. (Section 89501.) Specified local officials and designated employees of local agencies may not receive honoraria payments. (Section 89502.)

Slide 27, Bullet 2

It would be useful to show the definition of mass mailing so that your officials can be alert to avoid violating the law.

■ Mass mailing restrictions (GC § 89001) Goal: restrict incumbents’ advantages.

■ A mailing is prohibited by Section 89001 if all of the following criteria are met:

■ The item is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. The item delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

■ The item sent either: (A) features an elected officer affiliated with the agency, or (B) includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer.

■ (A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or (B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by this regulation.

■ More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request or other exception.

Slides 44-45

These slides set forth the eight-step analysis formerly applied to determine whether a public official is disqualified from taking part in a decision under Section 87100. In 2015, the Commission overhauled its conflict-of-interest regulations, abandoned this eight-step analysis, and replaced it with a four-step analysis set forth in Regulation 18700(d). Therefore, we recommend deleting these slides.

To the extent that you do want to discuss the 4-step process, we would advise it be addressed near/adjacent to Slide 14. Here’s some suggested language on the four-step process.

The image you provided is completely white, with no visible content or text. If you have another image or a different question, please provide it for better results.

Conflict-of-Interest Analysis Under the Political Reform Act (Regulation 18700(d))

■ Step One: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a financial effect on the public official’s financial interests? (Apply Regulation 18701.)
■ Step Two: Will the reasonably foreseeable financial effect be material? (Apply Regulation 18702.)
■ Step Three: Can the public official demonstrate that the material financial effect on the official’s financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally? (Apply Regulation 18703.)
■ Step Four: Is there an applicable exception? If not the official is prohibited from making, participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision. If disqualified, an official may be required to comply with the Act’s requirements for disqualification set forth in Regulation 18707.

We hope this information is of assistance. If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

embedding.qa_text: **STATE OF CALIFORNIA** **FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION** 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 August 2, 2016 Julie Chapman Legal Services Supervisor Mendocino County Counsel Office 501 Low Gap Road, Rm 1030 Ukiah, CA 95482 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-16-132 Dear Ms. Chapman: This letter responds to your request for assistance on behalf of Mendocino County regarding your required AB1234 training which includes provisions of Political Reform Act (the “Act”)¹ and Section 1090.² Please note that our assistance is limited to the provisions of the Act and Section 1090,² we do not review portions of the training that pertain to laws outside of our jurisdiction (See Regulation 18371), and we do not have any enforcement authority with respect to Section 53235 or other provisions of AB 1234. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.³ With respect to the other laws covered by the training (slides 1-7, 9-13, 16-17, 22, 24-33, 35-43, and 46-50), you may want to contact the Attorney General’s Office for their feedback. Please note that the Attorney General’s Office has its own requirements for AB1234 training.⁴ Discussion Section 53235(a), enacted by Assembly Bill No. 1234 (2005-2006 Reg. Sess.), requires certain local agency officials to receive training in ethics. Regulation 18371 provides that “the topics enumerated below . . . are considered to be the core content of the ‘ethics laws’ component that pertain to the Political Reform Act, and regarding which, the Commission will provide consultation. The course should include instruction on each of the following topics if relevant to the official’s public position: ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 and 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Section 1090 is not in the Act. However, in 2013, Assembly Bill 1090 granted the Commission the authority to provide written advice on Section 1090. ³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) ⁴ http://oag.ca.gov/sites/all/files/agweb/pdfs/ethics/eth_loc_guide_final.pdf? The image you provided is completely white, with no visible content or text.
